title: Matrescence: The Identity Rewrite No One Warns You About
subtitle: What happens to a woman's sense of self when she becomes a mother, and why it takes longer than anyone says
excerpt: A reading about matrescence: the psychological and identity transformation of becoming a mother. Covers why the old self does not simply continue, what the transition actually involves, and why the grief inside the joy is normal.
sourceNotes: Original to homemade.education. The term matrescence was coined by medical anthropologist Dana Raphael in 1973. Contemporary work by Dr Alexandra Sacks brought it into wider clinical and cultural use.

practice.whenToUse: Use when the identity shift of becoming a mother feels disorienting, or when you are carrying grief about the self that existed before and do not know where to put it.

content.text: When a person becomes a mother, the culture around them tends to focus on the baby. There is a great deal of attention paid to what the baby needs, what the baby is doing, and what the baby will do next. The mother, by comparison, is background: she is the context in which the new person is growing up.

This means that one of the most significant identity transformations a person can go through happens largely without acknowledgement. It has a name: matrescence. It does not yet have a cultural script.

Matrescence was named by anthropologist Dana Raphael in 1973. It describes the transition of becoming a mother: a shift that is practical, psychological, neurological, and identity-level. The word is deliberately parallel to adolescence. Both are periods of profound change. Both involve ambivalence, instability, and the slow emergence of a new self.

Unlike adolescence, matrescence has no coming-of-age ceremony. There is no shared ritual for the difficulty of it. The expectation is that the transition is mostly joyful, and that any ambivalence is a private problem to sort out quietly, not a normal feature of the experience.
The identity loss no one names

Before a woman becomes a mother, she has a self built over decades: preferences, habits, a sense of what her time is for, friendships built around who she was, a way of being in the world that is recognisably hers. When the baby arrives, much of this is disrupted or suspended. The body is different. Sleep is different. Time is different. The freedom to move through the world as an individual with individual needs is significantly reduced.

What many women report is that the old self does not simply continue, operating now with a child in it. The old self is changed. Some parts of it are lost. New parts emerge that were not there before. The problem is that the new self takes time to form, and in the meantime, the person is neither who she was nor who she will become.

One of the least-spoken features of early motherhood is the grief. Not depression, not ingratitude, but genuine grief for the life that has changed: the freedom, the spontaneity, the version of yourself that existed before. This grief can exist at the same time as deep love for the child. The two do not cancel each other out. They are both true.

The difficulty is that most cultural scripts for motherhood do not have room for this grief. The message, implicit and sometimes explicit, is that the love should be sufficient. That missing the former self is a sign that something is wrong with the mother or with how much she loves her child. Neither is true. The grief is a normal feature of a significant transition, not a character flaw.
The ambivalence that is also normal

Alongside the grief there is often ambivalence. Wanting and not-wanting at the same time. Love and frustration. Gratitude and resentment. Ambivalence about motherhood is common and rarely spoken about in the wider culture.

The suppression does not make the ambivalence go away. It makes it go underground, where it tends to collect and create pressure. Naming the ambivalence, even privately, reduces some of that pressure. The feeling is not the same as the action. Having the feeling does not make a person a bad mother.
The new self that is forming

Matrescence, like adolescence, does resolve. The disorientation is not permanent. Most women report that, eventually, a new version of themselves becomes recognisable: a self that includes the mother without being entirely defined by it, that has integrated what has been gained and what has been lost into a coherent identity.

This takes longer than anyone says. The first year is often described as the hardest. But the process continues well beyond the first year. There is no fixed point at which the transition is complete. The new self is built incrementally, out of hundreds of small moments of reclaiming parts of the former self, finding new capacities, and learning what kind of mother this particular person is going to be.
Original to homemade.education. The term matrescence was coined by Dana Raphael in 1973. Contemporary clinical work by Dr Alexandra Sacks brought it into wider cultural use.
